examples.title: Virtual Base Class - Diamond Problem Solution
examples.description: Complete program demonstrating virtual base class to solve diamond inheritance problem
examples.explanation: This program demonstrates virtual inheritance. Class B and C virtually inherit from A. Class D inherits from both B and C. With virtual inheritance, only one copy of A exists in D, eliminating the diamond problem. Constructor order: A (virtual base) → B → C → D (most-derived).

examples.title: Accessing Virtual Base Class Members
examples.description: Program showing how to resolve ambiguity when accessing base class members
examples.explanation: TeachingAssistant inherits from both Student and Teacher, which virtually inherit from Person. Only one Person object exists. The most-derived class TeachingAssistant initializes the virtual base Person. Access to name from Person is unambiguous because it's a single copy.

examples.title: Explicit Virtual Base Constructor Call
examples.description: Demonstrates how most-derived class explicitly calls virtual base constructor
examples.explanation: This shows that the most-derived class (MostDerived) is responsible for initializing the virtual base class (Base). Even though Derived1 and Derived2 try to initialize Base with different values, the MostDerived class constructor's initializer list determines the actual value (10 in this case).

examples.title: Size Comparison - Virtual vs Non-Virtual
examples.description: Program to compare object sizes with and without virtual inheritance
examples.explanation: This demonstrates the memory overhead of virtual inheritance. Without virtual: D1 contains two copies of A1. With virtual: D2 contains one A2 plus a vbptr (virtual base pointer), typically 4 or 8 bytes depending on platform. The virtual version may be larger due to the vbptr but eliminates data redundancy.
